chunk_summary: Age-specific activity, sedentary behavior, and sleep prescriptions are essential for managing metabolic risk in children, emphasizing physical activity, screen time limits, and sleep hygiene to improve metabolic outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand age-banded physical activity recommendations for children and adolescents.
Identify strategies to reduce sedentary behavior and recreational screen time in children with metabolic risk.
Learn pediatric sleep duration targets and their role in metabolic health.
Recognize the importance of screening for obstructive sleep apnea in children with metabolic risk.
key_facts: Children aged 5–17 years should engage in at least 60 minutes/day of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity, with vigorous activity and muscle/bone strengthening at least 3 days/week.
For children under 5 years, focus on active play in short bouts and reducing restrained time rather than structured exercise.
Recreational screen time should be limited to <2 hours/day, especially in youth with dysglycemia or MASLD.
School-age children need 9–12 hours of sleep per night, while adolescents require 8–10 hours, with consistent sleep-wake schedules.
Snoring or witnessed apneas should prompt evaluation for obstructive sleep apnea, as untreated sleep-disordered breathing worsens blood pressure, glycemia, and liver health.
reference_quote: Children aged 5–17 years should engage in at least 60 minutes/day of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity, with vigorous activity and muscle/bone strengthening at least 3 days/week. — WHO guidelines on physical activity and sedentary behaviour
document_key_topics: Sedentary behavior and screen time limits
Pediatric sleep hygiene and duration targets
Screening for obstructive sleep apnea in children
